title: Welcome
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Welcome

In this project, we'll be building a [Web Crawler](https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/bots/what-is-a-web-crawler/) in TypeScript using Node.js! To rank well in Google Search, websites need to [internally link pages](https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/links-crawlable#internal-links) one to another. For example, my blog post about the benefits of cheap haircuts should probably have a link to my other blog post about the best places to get haircuts.

We're going to write a TypeScript application that generates an "internal links" report for any website on the internet by crawling each page of the site.

## [!windows] for Windows Users

> [!warning]
> If you try to complete this course without [WSL 2](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) installed on Windows, you're gonna have a bad time.

## Learning Goals

- Get hands-on practice with local TypeScript development and tooling
- Practice making HTTP requests in TypeScript
- Learn how to parse HTML
- Practice unit testing

## TypeScript Setup

Before we dive into the project, let's make sure you are all set up properly. You will need:

1. A code editor. I use [VS code](https://code.visualstudio.com/), though you can use whatever you're comfortable with.
2. The [Boot.dev CLI](https://github.com/bootdotdev/bootdev#bootdev-cli) installed, and you'll need to be [logged in](https://github.com/bootdotdev/bootdev?tab=readme-ov-file#usage).
3. [NVM](https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm) installed.

## Installing Node.js With NVM

I recommend NVM because it allows you to have multiple installations of Node.js on the same machine. After installing NVM, add an `.nvmrc` file to the root of your project directory that contains a snippet of text:

```
22.15.0
```

This allows you to simply type `nvm use` in your CLI while in the root of your project to activate the correct version of node! You may get an installation command to run if you don't yet have that version of node, but it's just another one-liner.

## Assignment

Check to make sure you've activated the correct version of `node` by typing:

```sh
node --version
# Prints: v22.15.0
```

**Submit** the CLI tests. There's no penalty on failure for this lesson.

title: TypeScript Setup
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# TypeScript Setup

`npm` stands for "Node Package Manager". By installing Node.js, you've gained some new commands:

- [`node`](https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#command-line-api) - The JavaScript runtime. This will let you run your application files.
- [`npm`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v10/commands/npm) - The package manager. This manages dependencies, metadata, and allows you to specify "scripts" to run.
- [`npx`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/commands/npx) - The package runner. Run a command from a local or remote npm package.

**Most TS devs don't run the `node` commands directly, they use it within an npm script.**

## Assignment

1. [ ] Start a new node project with this command:

```sh
npm init
```

This command will prompt you with a series of questions. For this project, you can either press enter for the recommended defaults or follow the prompts.

2. [ ] Add TypeScript along with types for `node`, and [`tsx` (TypeScript Execute)](https://github.com/privatenumber/tsx) which will allow you to run TypeScript files directly in Node.js:

```sh
npm install -D typescript @types/node tsx
```

3. [ ] Create a `src` directory at the root of your project and create a file inside named `index.ts`. Add a `console.log` command to the file that prints `"Hello, World!"`. This file will be the entry point.

```ts
console.log("Hello, World!");
```

4. [ ] Configure TypeScript by creating a `tsconfig.json` file in the root of your project:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "esnext",
    "module": "esnext",
    "strict": true,
    "moduleResolution": "Node"
  },
  "include": ["./src/**/*.ts"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```

5. [ ] Edit the `package.json` file that was created during `npm init`. The scripts section should have something like:

```json
...
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "tsx ./src/index.ts"
  },
...
```

This enables your project to use [ES modules](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v13.x/api/esm.html#esm_ecmascript_modules) and adds a "start" script to this project that runs `index.ts` using the `tsx` typescript executable.

6. [ ] With all that ready to go, simply run:

```sh
npm start
```

And you should see your "hello world" message logged to the console!

## .gitignore

A [`.gitignore`](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/getting-started-with-git/ignoring-files) file is used to tell Git which files or folders to ignore when committing changes to a repository. This is useful for ignoring files that are specific to your local development environment or files that should not be tracked in the repository, such as compiled files or sensitive information.

7. [ ] Create a `.gitignore` file at the project root (with the leading period). Have git ignore the `node_modules/` directory.

**Submit** the CLI tests from **your project's root**. There's no penalty on failure for this lesson.

title: Normalize URLs
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Normalize URLs

[Test-driven development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Test-driven_development) is a popular method of writing software. The idea is that you write tests for your code _first_, then you write the code that gets the tests to pass. We're going to approach this project using _a bit of_ TDD.

## Normalizing URLs

We need a function that accepts a URL string as input and returns a "normalized" URL. To "normalize" means to "make the same". For example, all of these URLs are the "same page" according to most websites and HTTP standards:

- `https://www.boot.dev/blog/path/`
- `https://www.boot.dev/blog/path`
- `http://www.boot.dev/blog/path/`
- `http://www.boot.dev/blog/path`

We want our `normalizeURL()` function to map all of those same inputs to a single normalized output: `www.boot.dev/blog/path`.

_Keep in mind, the normalized URL isn't going to be used to make requests, it's just going to be used to compare URLs to see if they are the same page._

## Stub the Function

In [test driven development (TDD)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Test-driven_development), you typically follow this flow:

- Stub out the function
- Write the tests
- Write the code that passes the tests

You are going to be using the [Vitest](https://vitest.dev/guide/) testing library for TypeScript.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Install `vitest` in your project as a [dev dependency](https://medium.com/@stalonadsl948/dependencies-vs-devdependencies-926e096a3dee) using `npm`:

```sh
npm install -D vitest
```

2. [ ] Add a `test` script property to your `package.json` file:

```json
...
"scripts": {
  "start": "tsx ./src/index.ts",
  "test": "vitest run"
},
...
```

3. [ ] In our `src` directory, create a file called `crawl.ts` and create a function called `normalizeURL`. Make sure to [export](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/web/javascript/reference/statements/export) it from the module (file).

I'd recommend you read up on Vitest's [getting started guide here](https://vitest.dev/guide/#writing-tests). By default, the `vitest` command will look for files that end in `.test.ts`, so write your first tests in a file called `crawl.test.ts` in the `src` directory.

4. [ ] Write a few tests! Remember, this function should take a single URL (string) and return the normalized version of the URL as a string. This will help us later to detect whether two different URLs are the same _web page_, which is all our users care about. Try to test all the [edge-cases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edge_case) you can think of.
5. [ ] Run your tests with `npm run test`. This works because you added the "test" script to `package.json`. You should see some _failing tests_ now, which is expected because you haven't filled in the `normalizeURL` function yet.
6. [ ] Write the `normalizeURL` Function. I recommend using the [URL object](https://nodejs.org/api/url.html#url-strings-and-url-objects) from the Node API. It parses a URL string into a URL object with some properties that will be useful to you.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests from the **root of the module**.

title: Extract Page Content
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Extract Page Content Our web crawler will need to know how to read a page of [HTML](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/Getting_started_with_the_web/HTML_basics). Now that we can normalize URLs, let's start extracting actual content from web pages. A web scraper that only normalizes URLs isn't very useful - we need to parse HTML and extract the meaningful information. Let's start with just parsing the `

` and first `

` tags. For example, from this HTML page: ```html

Welcome to Boot.dev

Learn to code by building real projects.

This is the second paragraph.

``` We want to extract: - `

`: "Welcome to Boot.dev" - The first `

`: "Learn to code by building real projects." ## Assignment We'll use a third-party HTML parsing library called [JSDOM](https://github.com/jsdom/jsdom) to find and extract links. 1. [ ] Stub out the new `getHeadingFromHTML` function and write some tests. Here's the signature: ```ts function getHeadingFromHTML(html: string): string; ``` - `html` is an HTML string - In your tests make sure that the function returns the text content of the `

` tag if present, or the `

` tag as a fallback. - Returns an empty string if neither an `

` nor an `

` tag is found. 2. [ ] Install `jsdom` and its types: ```sh npm install jsdom npm install -D @types/jsdom ``` This will install `jsdom` as a "dependency" (as opposed to `vitest` which is a "devDependency" and was installed with the `-D` flag). "Dev dependencies" are not required to run your application, they're only required for development (like testing). Regular dependencies are required to run the program itself. I'll try not to give too many hints: you should go read the JSDOM docs! That said here are a few: - `import { JSDOM } from 'jsdom'` - `new JSDOM(htmlBody)` creates a new "document object model" - `dom.window.document.querySelectorAll('a')` returns an array of `` tag "anchor" elements 3. [ ] Implement the `getHeadingFromHTML` function. We've already installed it as a dependency, you can import it using: `import { JSDOM } from "jsdom";` 4. [ ] Create a new function called `getFirstParagraphFromHTML`. Here's the signature: ```ts function getFirstParagraphFromHTML(html: string): string; ``` - In your tests make sure that the tests return the text content of the _first_ `

` tag. - Returns an empty string if no `

` tag is found. > [!tip] > You may find that the first `

` tag doesn't always have the best or most useful results. I'd recommend searching for the `

` tag if it exists and find the first `

` tag within it, if it doesn't exist fallback to just > the first `

` tag. Here are some example test cases to get you started: ```ts test("getHeadingFromHTML basic", () => { const inputBody = `

Test Title

`; const actual = getHeadingFromHTML(inputBody); const expected = "Test Title"; expect(actual).toEqual(expected); }); test("getFirstParagraphFromHTML main priority", () => { const inputBody = `

Outside paragraph.

Main paragraph.

`; const actual = getFirstParagraphFromHTML(inputBody); const expected = "Main paragraph."; expect(actual).toEqual(expected); }); ``` 1. [ ] Make sure you have at least 3 test cases for each function you are testing! **Run and submit** the CLI tests from the **root of your module**.

title: Extract Links and Images
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme: # Extract Links and Images We need to extract links from the HTML both the links people can click AND the images that are displayed. This gives us a complete picture of what resources each page references. For example, this HTML page has both a link and an image: ```html Go to Boot.dev ``` We need to extract both `https://crawler-test.com` (from the link) and `/logo.png` (from the image). ## Assignment 1. [ ] Write tests for a new function called `getURLsFromHTML`. Here's the signature: ```ts function getURLsFromHTML(html: string, baseURL: string): string[]; ``` - `html` is an HTML string - `baseURL` is the _root_ URL of the website we're crawling. This will allow us to rewrite [relative URLs into absolute URLs](https://www.seoclarity.net/resources/knowledgebase/difference-relative-absolute-url-15325/). - It returns an _un-normalized_ list of all the URLs found within the HTML, and an error if one occurs. In your tests, make sure that: - relative URLs are converted to absolute URLs - you find _all_ the `` tags in a body of HTML Here's one example test case to give you an idea: ```ts test("getURLsFromHTML absolute", () => { const inputURL = "https://crawler-test.com"; const inputBody = `Boot.dev`; const actual = getURLsFromHTML(inputBody, inputURL); const expected = ["https://crawler-test.com/path/one"]; expect(actual).toEqual(expected); }); ``` 2. [ ] Implement the `getURLsFromHTML` function. Here are a few hints you may find useful: - [`querySelectorAll()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Document/querySelectorAll) returns all elements that match the given selector. - You can use the `.get()` method to retrieve the value of a specific attribute. - [`getAttribute()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Element/getAttribute) returns element's attribute or null if there is no such attribute. - In HTML, "anchor" elements are links. With their `href` attribute containing the actual URL. e.g: ```html Learn Backend Development ``` 3. [ ] Write tests for a new function called `getImagesFromHTML`. Here's the signature: ```ts function getImagesFromHTML(html: string, baseURL: string): string[]; ``` - `html` is an HTML string - `baseURL` is the _root_ URL of the website we're crawling. This will allow us to rewrite [relative URLs into absolute URLs](https://www.seoclarity.net/resources/knowledgebase/difference-relative-absolute-url-15325/). - It returns an _un-normalized_ list of all the image URLs found within the HTML, and an error if one occurs. In your tests, make sure: - relative URLs are converted to absolute URLs - Handle cases where attributes might be missing Here's one example test case to give you an idea: ```ts test("getImagesFromHTML relative", () => { const inputURL = "https://crawler-test.com"; const inputBody = ``; const actual = getImagesFromHTML(inputBody, inputURL); const expected = ["https://crawler-test.com/logo.png"]; expect(actual).toEqual(expected); }); ``` 4. [ ] Implement the `getImagesFromHTML` function. 5. [ ] Make sure you have at least 3 test cases for each function you're testing. **Run and submit** the CLI tests from the **root of your module**.

title: Structure Page Data
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript

title: Main
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Main

Let's get to work on the actual application! Our program takes as a [command line argument](https://nodejs.org/en/learn/command-line/accept-input-from-the-command-line-in-nodejs) the website URL we want to crawl (or at least the page we want to start from)

Remember, you can run the program itself using `npm`:

```sh
npm run start BASE_URL
```

Where `BASE_URL` is the root URL of the website we want to crawl. For example: `https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/`.

Create a main function and call it.

```js
function main() {
  // ?
}

main();
```

You will need to [process command line arguments](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/process.html#processargv) for this step.

All your main function should do at this point is:

1. [ ] If the number of CLI arguments is less than 1, print an error and `process.exit` with code 1.
2. [ ] If the number of CLI arguments is more than 1, print an error and `process.exit` with code 1.
3. [ ] If we have exactly one CLI argument, it's the "baseURL", so print some kind of message letting the user know the crawler is starting at that baseURL and then `process.exit` with code 0.

> [!warning]
> The number of arguments we pass to `npm` is not equal to the number of arguments to our CLI.

## Test It by Running It

This is a good example of some code that's a bit weird to test with unit tests. Just run the dang thing with too few, just the right amount, and too many arguments to ensure it behaves as expected.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests from the **root of the project**.

title: Get HTML
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Get HTML

Let's start crawling real web pages! For these remaining steps, use `https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/`. A single product page like `https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/products/ashenfang-longsword-fan-1001/` is a good place to start.

## Assignment

Create an async `getHTML` function in `crawl.ts`. For now, it will just take a URL (in this case, the root of the site we're going to crawl).

```ts
async function getHTML(url: string);
```

For now, your function should:

1. [ ] Use [fetch](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Fetch_API) to fetch the webpage of the `url`
   - [ ] Set a `User-Agent` header (e.g. `BootCrawler/1.0`) to avoid being blocked by servers.
2. [ ] If the HTTP status code is an error-level code (400+), print an error and return
3. [ ] If the response `content-type` header is not `text/html` print an error and return
4. [ ] Otherwise, just print the HTML body as a string and be done

Remember to use `try/catch` as appropriate for anything that _could_ result in an error!

5. [ ] Import `getHTML` into your main function, and call it with the `base_url` passed in. Give your program a shot! It should print some HTML that it fetched from the internet!

**Run and submit** the CLI tests from the **root of the project**.

title: Adding Recursion
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Adding Recursion

This is going to be the largest step so far, and will require the most "figuring it out on your own"... **you got this**.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a `crawlPage` function that [uses recursion](https://www.boot.dev/blog/javascript/how-to-recursively-traverse-objects/) to traverse a website. Recursion is useful because we want to continue to crawl each URL we find on a page until we've crawled every page on the site. Here's my function signature:

```ts
async function crawlPage(
  baseURL: string,
  currentURL: string,
  pages: Record<string, number>,
);
```

- `currentURL` is the _current_ URL we're crawling.
- `baseURL` is the _root_ URL of the website we're crawling.

In the first call to `crawlPage()`, `currentURL` will just be a copy of the `baseURL`, but as we make further fetch requests to all the URLs we find on the `baseURL`, the `currentURL` value will change while the base stays the same.

The `pages` object will be used to keep track of the number of times we've seen each internal link. This function needs to always return an updated version of this object.

This is a good use case for default parameters:

```ts
function crawlPage(
  baseURL: string,
  currentURL: string = baseURL,
  pages: Record<string, number> = {},
);
```

Here's my pseudocode:

- Make sure the `currentURL` is on the same domain as the `baseURL`. If it's not, just return the current `pages`. We don't want to crawl the entire internet, just the domain in question.
- Get a normalized version of the `currentURL`.
- If the `pages` object already has an entry for the normalized version of the current URL, just increment the count and return the current `pages`.
- Otherwise, add an entry to the `pages` object for the normalized version of the current URL, and set the count to `1`.
- Get the HTML from the current URL using our `getHTML` function, and add a print statement so you can watch your crawler in real-time.
- Assuming all went well with the `fetch` request in the new function, get all the URLs from the response body HTML.
- Recursively crawl each URL you found on the page and update the `pages` to keep an aggregate count.
- Finally, return the updated `pages` object.

> [!danger]
> Be careful testing this! Be sure to add print statements so you can see what your crawler is doing, and **kill it with `ctrl+c` if it's stuck in a loop**. If you make too many spammy requests to a website (including the sandbox) you could get your IP address blocked.

2. [ ] Call `crawlPage` in the main function instead of `getHTML` and log the returned pages.
3. [ ] Test your program by running it against `https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/`.
   ```sh
   npm run start https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/
   ```
4. [ ] When you're satisfied that everything is working, you can move on.

title: Concurrency
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Concurrency

Your web crawler works – but it's crawling pages one at a time. It would take us a really long time to crawl a large website. Let's make it faster using [Promise-based concurrency](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Promise). This is another long step, but don't get discouraged!

## Assignment

We will be using the [p-limit](https://www.npmjs.com/package/p-limit) package to help us add concurrency to our project.

1. [ ] Install the `p-limit` package:

   ```sh
   npm install p-limit
   ```

   - Follow the instructions in the [p-limit documentation](https://www.npmjs.com/package/p-limit) to import the package.

2. [ ] Create a class named `ConcurrentCrawler` to manage shared state for your crawler. Create a constructor to initialize the following fields:
   - `baseURL` (the starting URL)
   - `pages` (our Record of page visit counts)
   - `limit` (a function created with `pLimit(maxConcurrency)`)

3. [ ] Add a method named `addPageVisit` to your `ConcurrentCrawler`:

   ```ts
   private addPageVisit(normalizedURL: string): boolean
   ```

   - Take a normalized URL
   - Update the pages object like you did before
   - Return true if it's the first time visiting the page, otherwise return false

4. [ ] Make your old `getHTML` function a method of `ConcurrentCrawler`. Keep the same error, status, and header checks:

   ```ts
   private async getHTML(currentURL: string): Promise<string>
   ```

   - Wrap the same fetch logic and error handling we had before in our new `limit` function and return the result:
     ```ts
     return await this.limit(async () => {
       // same fetch logic and error handling as before
       // return res.text() here
     });
     ```

   Wrapping our `getHTML` logic inside the `limit` function, gives us control over how many concurrent fetches run at once. This can be adjusted by changing the value of `maxConcurrency`. Keeping this low helps us avoid hammering the target server with too many requests.

5. [ ] Update your old `crawlPage` function and make it a method of `ConcurrentCrawler`:

   ```ts
   private async crawlPage(currentURL: string): Promise<void>
   ```

   - Call your new `addPageVisit` method, if it is not a new page return early
   - Get the HTML from the current URL using your new `getHTML` method
   - Get all the URLs from the response body HTML
   - Create an array of promises for each URL by calling `this.crawlPage(nextURL)`
   - Use `Promise.all()` to await all the concurrent crawl promises

6. [ ] Add a method named `crawl` to `ConcurrentCrawler` that starts by calling `crawlPage` with the `baseURL` and returns the pages

7. [ ] Create a function named `crawlSiteAsync` that:
   - Creates a `ConcurrentCrawler` instance
   - Calls and awaits the `crawl()` method
   - Returns the final pages Record

8. [ ] **Test it (I tested it manually again).** I recommend setting your `maxConcurrency` to 1 to start to just make sure everything still works. Then bump it up to 3, 5, or 10 to see it run faster.

## Tips

- Make sure you're _not_ crawling the same page multiple times. That's why my `addPageVisit` method returns a boolean: to indicate if it's the first time we've seen the page.
- Ensure you are only crawling URLs that belong to the same domain.
- Use `Promise.all()` to handle multiple concurrent requests efficiently

title: Max Pages
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Max Pages

As it stands, our crawler is only useful on fairly small sites. Sites with thousands or _hundreds of thousands_ of pages take too long to crawl.

_Google's crawlers run concurrently on fleets of servers to crawl the majority of the internet. You might not have that kind of budget for this project._

Let's add a `maxPages` setting so that we can crawl even gigantic websites and have our tool automatically stop when it's done a reasonable amount of work.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add a `maxPages: number` field to your `ConcurrentCrawler` class. This will be the maximum number of unique pages to crawl.
2. [ ] Add a `shouldStop: boolean` field and an `allTasks: Set<Promise<void>>` to your `ConcurrentCrawler` to help coordinate stopping all concurrent tasks.
3. [ ] When creating new crawl tasks, add them to the `allTasks` set, and remove them when they complete. This can be done with [`.finally()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Promise/finally).
4. [ ] Update your `addPageVisit` method to properly stop the crawler when the limit is reached:
   - [ ] Check if `shouldStop` is already `true` and return `false` immediately if so.
   - [ ] Check if the number of unique pages visited has reached `maxPages`. If so:
     - Set `shouldStop = true`
     - Print `"Reached maximum number of pages to crawl."`
     - Return `false`
5. [ ] Update your `crawlPage` function to check `shouldStop` at the very beginning and return immediately if it's `true`.
6. [ ] Make both the `maxConcurrency` and `maxPages` settings configurable via command-line arguments.
7. [ ] Now you should be able to run your program with this syntax:
   ```sh
   # usage: npm run start <URL> <maxConcurrency> <maxPages>
   npm run start https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/ 3 10
   ```

Make sure that your program prints a line to the console each time you crawl a page, as well as once per page in the report. This will help you see what your crawler is doing, and ensure you can kill it with ctrl+c if it's stuck in a loop or spamming requests.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Collecting Page Data
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Collecting Page Data

Now we have a crawler that can run efficiently, with options to tune concurrency and set a limit on the number of pages to crawl.

But you may have noticed that what the crawler is doing isn't very useful. It recursively fetches pages and... increments counters to record how many times it hit each page? What's the point of that?

Fortunately, we already wrote the function that we need in order to have our crawler collect actual data: `extractPageData`, from back in the first chapter.

What we need to do now is to update the `pages` member of our `ConcurrentCrawler` class, so that it's a map of URL strings to _page data objects_ – we have an `ExtractedPageData` type for this – rather than to numbers of visits. i.e.,

```ts
pages: Record<string, ExtractedPageData>;
```

This way, when we call our crawler, it will return a data structure that tells us both what URLs it found and some key details about each of them.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Update the type of `pages` to `Record<string, ExtractedPageData>`.
2. [ ] Remove the logic that increments a counter when a page is visited; it's no longer relevant (or even correct, given the type change).
3. [ ] In the recursive crawling function, remove the direct call to `getURLsFromHTML`. Replace it with a call to `extractPageData`, and assign the return value to a variable, e.g.:
   ```ts
   const data = extractPageData(html, currentURL);
   ```
4. [ ] After extracting the page data, save it to `pages[normalizedURL]`. This is the key part where we're putting actual data about each page in the `pages` map!
5. [ ] Since we called `extractPageData` instead of `getURLsFromHTML`, when we go to recurse, we need to iterate over the `outgoing_links` property of the page data we collected, e.g.:
   ```ts
   for (const nextURL of data.outgoing_links)
   ```
6. [ ] Update any function signatures that still have the old type of `pages` (`Record<string, number>`). If you miss any, the TypeScript compiler may catch them for you...
7. [ ] Back in the main script file, when you call `await crawlSiteAsync()`, make sure the result is assigned to a variable, e.g. `pages`. (You may already have it this way.)
8. [ ] After the crawler runs, add the following console output, which reports that crawling is finished and verifies that appropriate page data exists for the first page that was visited:
   ```ts
   console.log("Finished crawling.");
   const firstPage = Object.values(pages)[0];
   if (firstPage) {
     console.log(
       `First page record: ${firstPage["url"]} - ${firstPage["heading"]}`,
     );
   }
   ```

Try running the crawler on `https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/`. If everything looks right, then **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: JSON Report
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# JSON Report

We're almost done! Our web crawler now extracts rich data from every page and stores it efficiently in an object. Let's export it to **JSON** so it's easy to read and share.

For example, one page record in that object might look like this:

```ts
export const pageData: Record<string, ExtractedPageData> = {
  "learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/products/ashenfang-longsword-fan-1001":
    {
      url: "https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/products/ashenfang-longsword-fan-1001/",
      heading: "Ashenfang Longsword",
      first_paragraph:
        "A balanced battlefield blade with a smoldering fuller and leather-wrapped grip.",
      outgoing_links: [
        "https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/",
        "https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/categories/",
        "https://learnwebscraping.dev/practice/ecommerce/categories/longswords/",
      ],
      image_urls: [
        "https://learnwebscraping.dev/images/catalog/longswords.svg",
      ],
    },
};
```

We want to create a `report.json` file containing a sorted array of all page records.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new file `report.ts` and export a `writeJSONReport` function. Here's the signature:

   ```ts
   export function writeJSONReport(
     pageData: Record<string, ExtractedPageData>,
     filename = "report.json",
   ): void;
   ```

   - `pageData` is the object returned by your crawler
   - `filename` is the JSON file to create (defaults to "report.json")

2. [ ] Implement `writeJSONReport`. It should:
   - Sort the pages by `url` for deterministic output
   - Serialize the sorted array with [`JSON.stringify`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/JSON/stringify) using 2-space indentation
   - Write the file to disk using [`fs.writeFileSync`](https://nodejs.org/api/fs.html#fswritefilesyncfile-data-options)

Here are some tips:

- Sort: `const sorted = Object.values(pageData).sort((a, b) => a.url.localeCompare(b.url))`
- Serialize: `JSON.stringify(sorted, null, 2)`
- Resolve path: `path.resolve(process.cwd(), filename)`

3. [ ] Update your `index.ts`:
   - Import `writeJSONReport` from `./report`
   - Call `writeJSONReport(pages, "report.json")` after crawling completes

4. [ ] Test your implementation:
   - Verify `report.json` is created after running the crawler
   - Check that it contains a valid JSON array of page objects

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Submit the Link to Your Repository!
courseTitle: Build a Web Scraper in TypeScript
readme:
# Submit the Link to Your Repository!

If you haven't been keeping track of your project in Git, you should be! Once you have your finished project on GitHub, submit the link to your project in the text box to the right! Your link should look something like this: `https://github.com/github-username/repo-name`.

I hope you've enjoyed the project, it's always good to solidify the things you learn in courses by getting your hands dirty with a real project.

## Ideas for Extending the Project

- Make the script run on a timer and deploy it to a server. Have it email you every so often with a report
- Add more robust error checking so that you can crawl larger sites without issues
- Count external links, as well as internal links, and add them to the report
- Use a graphics library to create an image that shows the links between the pages as a graph visualization
- Make requests concurrently to speed up the crawling process
- Add a `README.md` file explaining to users how to clone your git repo and get started

## Contact Me

I hang out in the [Boot.dev Discord](https://boot.dev/community) and on [Twitter @wagslane](https://twitter.com/wagslane). Let me know what you thought of the project!
